Farn Por-rrrcAr- PnACTrces C oMMrssroN
-128 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacrarnento. CA 9-581-l-2-129
(916) 322--5660 r Fax (916) 321-0886
October 6,2OlO
Karl H. Berger
Deputy City Attorney
City of Monterey Park
Jenkins & Hogin, LLP
1230 Rosecrans Avenue, Suite I l0
Manhattan Beach, CA 90266
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-10-153
Dear Mr. Berger:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Nothing in this letter should be constmed to
evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this
letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented.
The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact
when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is
general in nature and you have not limited your question to a specific governmental decision, we
are treating your reqllest as one for informal assistance.2
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore
offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-ot--interest laws such as common law
conflict of interest and Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTTONS
l. After 12 months have elapsed from the date he is appointed as city manager, may Mr.
Talbot, who is currently a fifty-percent owner of a consulting business representing the developer
' The Political Reform Act is contained in GovernmentCode Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections ltS l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the Calitirrnia Code of Regulations, unless ritherwise indicated.
r Infirrmal assistance does not provide the requestur with the immunity provided by an opinion or turmal
written advice. (Section 831 14, Regulation 18329(cX-l), copy enclosed.)


File No. I-10-153
Page No. 2
of the Market Place Development, take part in decisions regarding the development if he divests
himself of any interest in the consulting business immediately upon his appointment?
2. Upon his appointment, may Mr. Talbot take part in decisions related to other private
developments or the merging of.two redevelopment project areas that may affect the amount of
public funds available for the Market Place Development?
3. If the City of Monterey Park (the "City") determines before Mr. Talbot is appointed
city manager that the City will not spend funds on the Market Place Development for ii least 12
months after Mr. Talbot's appointment, may Mr. Talbot take part in decisions upon his
appointment related to other private developments or the merging of two redevelopment project
areas that may affect the amount of public funds available for the Market Place Development
CONCLUSIONS
1. Provided there are no reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on any other
economic interests Mr. Talbot may have, he may take part in decisions regarding the Market
Place Development after the elapse of 12 months from his appointrnent ro long as he divests
himself of any interest in his consulting business immediately upon his appointment and receives
no remuneration of any kind from either the business or the developer of the project after his
appointment.
2. Upon his appointment, Mr. Talbot may not take part in decisions related to other
private projects or the merging of two redevelopment project areas that may affect the amount of
public funds available for the Market Place Development, within l2 monthi of receiving income
from either his former business or the developer of the Market Place Development, unless the
decisions can be segmented as discussed below.
3. Even if the City determines before Mr. Talbot is appointed city manager that it will
not spend funds on the Market Place Development for at least 12 months after Mr. Talbot,s
appointment, Mr. Talbot may not take part in decisions related to other private projects or the
merging of two redevelopment project areas that may affect the amouniof pubfic funds available
for the Market Place Development, within 12 months of receiving income fiom either his former
business or the developer of the Market Place Development, unless the decisions can be
segmented as discussed below.
FACTS
The City is considering individuals to hire as its city manager/execLltive director. One
candidate for this position is Mr. Talbot. You seek assistance as the authorized representative of
the City, Mr. Talbot, and the Monterey park Redevelopment Agency (the ..RDA,,j.
Through its city council, the City created the RDA in 1972. Currently, the RDA oversees
two redevelopment project areas known as the Atlantic Garvey Project Area and the Merged


File No. I-10-153
Page No. 3
Project Area. Additionally, the Merged Project Area includes three major private developments
known as the Market Place Development, the Towne Center Project, ,nA tt " Atlantic Times
Square Project.
Mr. Talbot is currently a fifty-percent equity partner in a business named Gateway
Consultants ("Gateway"), which provides development consulting services. Currently, dateway
is providing consulting services to Monterey Park Retail Partners, LLC ("MpRp,,), *-hich is the
developer for the Market Place Development. The Market Place Development consists of a
planned 600,000 square foot retail shopping center on a forty-five acre siie located within
Monterey Park. Additionally, MPRP has an option to purchase the real property upon which the
Market Place Development is proposed to be built. MPRP is seeking a number of intitlements
and agreements with the City and the RDA including, without limitaiion, environmental review
in accordance with the Califomia Environmental Quality Act, processing subdivision maps, a
development agreement with the City, and a disposition and develop-"nt agreement with the
RDA. At this time, none of these entitlements have been approved. The eniitlements are being
processed and will potentially be considered by the City and the RDA within the next 12 months.
The RDA and MPRP have also previously discussed the feasibility of paying for certain
offsite improvements, such as roadway improvements, located adjacent to the Market place
Development. These improvements will provide benefits to the Market Place Development as
well as other adjacent properties. If the RDA does not build the offsite improvements, MpRp
will need to pay for the improvements, which are estimated to cost uppror,i-otely $g,000,000.
While uncertain, it is also possible that decisions related to the Atlaniic Times Square project and
the Towne Center Project may affect the amount of money available for the offsiie
improvements needed for the Market Place Development. To address monetary limitations, the
city is contemplating merging the Atlantic Garvey Project Area with the Merged project Area.
This merger would make more funds available for projects within the newly lormed project area
and provide greater flexibility regarding the use of redevelopment monies.
During the previous 12 months, MPRP has paid Gateway more than $500 for consulting
services. Payments are made on a monthly basis, and you have asked that we presume Mr.
Talbot has received over $500 from Gateway for services provided to MPRp. -you 
,tut" that Mr.
Talbot is not entitled to a completion bonus upon the completion of the Market place
Development. If hired as the city manager, Mr. Talbot has informed the City that he will
immediately resign from his position at Gateway. Upon his resignation, Mr. Talbot will not
receive any further remuneration from the MPRP. In a telephon" "orr"rration on September 21,
2010, you stated that, in resigning from his position with Gateway, Mr. Talbot will divest
himself of any interest he holds in Gateway including his fifty-peicent ownership interest.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her otTicial position to influence a governmental deciiion in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within


File No. I-10-153
Page No.4
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.
Step One: Is the individual a "public official?,,
The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bX1).) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .." (Section 82048.) If apyointed as the
city manager, Mr. Talbot will be a public official within the meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Is the official making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any collrse of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.\ A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the
official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) If hired as the city manager, Mr. Talbot will be making,
participating in making, or influencing governmental decisions when taking part in cit! decisions
regarding MPRP, private developments within the city, or redevelopment projects.
Step Three: What are the official's economic interests?
Of the economic interests recognized under the Acta, those interests that may be
implicated by your account of the facts are the following:
Business Entity - A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which
he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or she i.s a
I If a public official's otfice is listed in Section 81200 (*87200 filers" include city managers) and he or she
has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: ( I ) immediately prior to the
discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of
the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation 18702.5(bX I )(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself
or herselt and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. Foi closed sessions,
consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member ofthe public regarding personal interests, special rules tbund
in Regulation 18702.5. subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)
' our analysis is limited to the economic interests you have identit'ied.


File No. I-10-153
Page No. 5
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section
87103(a) and (d); Regulation 18703.1(a) and (b).)
Source of Income - A public official has an economic interest in any source of income,
including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within the 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) "Income" is defined to include a pro rata
share of the income of any business entity or trlrst in which the official (or his or her ipouse)
owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest. (Section SZdfOlay.y
Personal Finances - A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the "personal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 1g703.5.)
Provided that Mr. Talbot immediately divests himself of any interesr in Gateway if hired
as the city manager, no economic interests are implicated by your accollnt of the facts for
decisions more than 12 months from Mr. Talbot's appointment. Barring any other economic
interests that Mr. Talbot may have, the Act does not prohibit Mr. Talboi from making,
participating in making, or influencing a decision by the City or the RDA affecting either
Gateway or MPRP, after 12 months of leaving Gateway, so long as he has received no income
from either source during the l2-month period.
Notwithstanding decisions made more than 12 months after Mr. Talbot has divested
himself of his interest in Gateway, you have also asked for assistance regarding Mr. Talbot,s
participation in decisions upon his appointment that may affect Gateway or nAFnp by affecting
the amount of public funds available for the Market Place Developmeri. Th.r" decisions
potentially include decisions regarding the Atlantic Times Square Project, the Towne Center
Project, or the merging of the Atlantic-Garvey Project Area with the Merged project Area. We
continue our analysis in regards to Mr. Talbot's participation in decisio6 upo., his appointment
but within 12 months of divesting himself of his interest in Gateway
Gateway and MPRP:
Provided that Mr. Talbot divests himself of any interest in Gateway immediately upon his
appointment, he will not have an investment interest in Gateway. Howevir, from the facts
provided, Mr. Talbot has received income of $500 dollars or more from Gateway for services
provided to MPRP. Thus, Mr. Talbot has an economic interest in Gateway as a source of
income. Additionally, because Mr. Talbot held a fifty-percent ownership interest in Gateway
prior to divesting his interest, any source of income to Gateway is also asource of income to Mr.
Talbot if his pro rata share of the income derived by Gateway from the source aggregates to $500
or more in the l2 months prior to the govemmental decision. (Sections 82030(a) -a gZtOl("1.)
Based Llpon the facts provided, Mr. Talbot also has an economic interest in MpRp as a source of
income.


File No. I-10-153
Page No. 6
Personal Finances:
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances. A
governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will result in
the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regularion 18703.5.)
Step Four: Are the official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
Gateway and MPRP:
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source oi in"o*e,
either directly or by an agent:
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject oia
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."
Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in governmental
decisions are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 18704.1(b), 18705.1(a)(2), and
Regulation 18705.3(b).) Under the facts you have presented, there is no indication that either
Gateway or MPRP are directly involved in decisions other than decisions related to the Market
Place Development.
Personal Finances
As for an official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, an official's
economic interest in his or her personal finances is deemed to be directly involved in the
governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect on the economic interest. (Regulation
18704.5.)


File No. I-10-153
Page No. 7
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
official's economic interests?
Materiality
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to each economic interest.
Gateway and MpRp:
For economic interests in business entities directly involved in a decision, including
business entities that are a source of income to an officiai, the materiality standard is given at
Regulation 18705.1(b), which states, "the financial effects of a govemmlnt decision on a
business entity which is directly involved in the govemment deJision is presumed to be
material." This presumption of materiality *ay be rebutted only by p.oof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have aiy-financial effect on the
business entity.
For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision, including
business entities that are a source of income to an official, ihe materiality standard is given at
Regulation 18705.1(c). The thresholds for materiality under this regulaiion vary with the size of
the business. While you have not provided 
-facts relaiing to the size of either Gateway or MpRp,
Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) provides that the financial .fflt of a governmental decision on a
business, not publicly traded and relatively modest in economic size, is material if it is
reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing.*f"nr". fo. o
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 o, *or"; or,
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,
Mr' Talbot should consult Regulation 18705.1(c) to ensure that he identifies the
materiality threshold actually appropriate to both cateway and MpRp.
In addition to the materiality standards for sources of income described above, there is a
separate materiality standard that applies in cases where there is a ..nexus,, between duties owed
to a source of income and to the official's public agency. when a public official is paid by


File No. I-10-153
Page No. 8
another entity to accomplish some action that is within the official's public decisionmaking
authority, any financial effect on the solrrce of income is presumed material. The .,nexlls test,, is
set out at Regulation 18705.3(c), which provides the following:
"(c) Nexus. Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who
is a source of income to a public official is deemed material if the pubtic official
receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose *t i"t would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."
Personal Finances:
Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in Regulation 1g705.5(a),
which provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal
finances is materiar if it is at least $250 in any l2-montL period. when
determining whether a governmental decisio, hor o material financial effect on a
public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a
financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the
official, nor a financial effect on the g.o.r i.re.rues, expenses, or value of assets
and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered."
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each of his or
her economic interests, the next step is determining whethei it is "reason'ably foreseeable,, that
the standard will be met. A material financial erre"t on an economic interesi is ..reasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met
as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) However, an effect need not be
certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility.
(ln re Tlnrner (1975) 1 FppC Ops. l9g.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Tltorner, sypra,at l9g.)
Because the Commission does not act as. a finder of fact in providing advice (ln re oglesby
(r975) I FPPC ops. 7l), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determinarion that
must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.
Nonetheless' you have provided facts indicating that decisions regarding the Market
Place Development will have a reasonably foreseeable-material financial effect on Mr. Talbot,s
economic interests in both Gateway and MPRP as sources of income. First, both Gateway and
MPRP appear to be directly involved in decisions regarding the Market place Development.
Second, there also appears to be a nexlrs between ur. rauit's duties as a consultant and duties


File No. I-10-153
Page No. 9
should he be hired as the city manager. Thus, the financial effect on Mr. Talbot,s economic
interests in Gateway and MPRP is presumed material under both Regulations 1g705.1(b) and
18705'3(c). Even baring any other economic interests, Mr. Talbot may not make, participate in
making, or influen^ce the decisions regarding the Market Place Develoirnent unless he can rebut
the presumption of materiality by demonstrating that there will be absolutely zo financial effect,
not even a single-penny's effect, on his economic interests in either Gateway or MpRp.
Additionally, you have stated that decisions related to the Atlantic Times square project
and the Towne Center Project, as well as the merging of the Atlantic Garvey project Area with
the Merged Project Area, may affect the amount of puUf i" funds available for the Market place
Development' Based upon these facts, it also appears that decisions related to these two projects,
and the merging of the redevelopment project oreur, will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Mr. Talbot's economic-interests in both Gateway ana vtpnp as sources of
income' As a previously paid consultant for the Market Place Development, any financial effect
on Mr' Talbot's economic interests in Gateway or MPRP as sources of income is deemed
material under the nexus test because he has received income in the previous 12 months to
achieve a goal or purpose that would be achieved, defeated, aided, oi hindered by decisions
related to these two projects or the merging of the project areas. Even barring any other
economic interests, Mr. Talbot may not make, particiiate in making, or influJnce the decisions
related to the Atlantic Times Square Project and the To*n" Center-iro.lect, as well as the
merging of the Atlantic Garvey Project Area with the Merged project Area, unless (l) he can
rebut the presumption of materiality by demonstrating that'there will be absolutely no financial
effect, not even a single-penny's effect, on his ,"onoiri" interests in either Gateway or MpRp or
!?]^.1":*.1:l:iti* segmented from decisions regarding the Marker place Devetopment as
orscusseo Delow."
Steps Seven and Eight: Does the governmental decision come within any exception to the
confl ict-of-interest rules?
Segmentation
The commission has advised that "[u]nder certain circumstances, a public official
disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions provided that the
official's participation does not affect the decision in which he or she has a conflict of interest.,,
(In re owen (1976) 2 FPPC ops. 77.) [n these cases, related decisions -uy U" r"gmented from
the decision in which the official has a conflict of interest so long as the o.iion taken in the
5 unless segmented.from.decisions regarding the Market Place Development, Mr. Talbot is also likely
prohibited from making, participating in making, or irifluencing decisions related ro rhe Arlantic Times square
Project and the Towne Center Project, as well ai the merging o'f the Atiantic Garvey project Area with the Merged
Project Area, solely becauseof his likely disqualification t.J* decisions regardingihe Market place Developmenr.
In the past, the Commission has advised that i'inextricably intenelated" decisions Lay not be considered separately
unless the decisions can be segmented, as discussed below. tsee generail y yangAdvice Letter, No. I-06- l9g ; stone
Advice Lerter, No. A.-06-007; Barker Advice Letter, A_05; and HullAdvice Letrer, No. 4_04_052.)


File No. I-10-153
Page No. 10
related decision will not effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the decision
in which the official has a conflict of interest.
Regulation 18709 provides the rules for the "segmentation" of a governmental decision:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, prorided all of the
following conditions apply:
"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented
from the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's participation in any way; and
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has
been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a
reopening of or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official
was disqualified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably
interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively determinl, affirm,
nullify, or alter the result of another decision.',
Based upon the facts you have provided, it may be possible to segment decisions related
to the Atlantic Times Square Project and the Towne Centei Project, o. *.ll as the merging of the
Atlantic Garvey Project Area with the Merged Project Area, from decisions regarding ihe-trrtarket
Place Development. However, this is a viable option only to the extent that de-cisionJ regarding
the Market Place Development are considered first, without Mr. Talbot's participation, ild
ftiture decisions regarding the other projects, and the merging of the redevelopment project areas,
will not act to determine, affirm, nullify, or alter a previously made decision ielated to the
Market Place Development. If you need additional assistance regarding the segmentation of a
specific governmental decision, it is advisable that you seek further advice.
Public Generally & Legally Required participation
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is


File No. I-10-153
Page No. 11
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally. (Section
87103; Regulation I 8707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take part
in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the
"legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very specific
circumstances where the govemment agency would bi paratyzed from acting. (section g7101;
Regulation 18708.)
However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally,, or the
"le.gally required participation" exceptions are applicable to Mr. Talbot-,s circu-mstances, so we
will not address them further.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.conclusion:
1. Provided there are no reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on any other
economic interests Mr. Talbot may have, he may take part in decisions regarding the Market
Place Development after the elapse of 12 months from his appointrnent ro long as he divests
himself of any interest in his consulting business immediately upon his appointment and receives
no remuneration of any kind from either the business or the developer of the project after his
appointment.
2. Upon his appointment, Mr. Talbot may not take part in decisions related to other
private projects or the merging of two redevelopment project areas that may affect the amount of
public funds available for the Market Place Development, within l2 monthi of receiving income
from either his former business or the developer of the Market Place Development, unless the
decisions can be segmented as discussed below.
3. Even if the City determines before Mr. Talbot is appointed city manager that it will
not spend funds on the Market Place Development for at least 12 months after Mr. Talbot,s
appointment, Mr. Talbot may not take part in decisions related to other private projects or the
merging of two redevelopment project areas that may affect the amouniof pubfic funds available

sections.facts:
The City is considering individuals to hire as its city manager/execLltive director. One
candidate for this position is Mr. Talbot. You seek assistance as the authorized representative of
the City, Mr. Talbot, and the Monterey park Redevelopment Agency (the ..RDA,,j.
Through its city council, the City created the RDA in 1972. Currently, the RDA oversees
two redevelopment project areas known as the Atlantic Garvey Project Area and the Merged
Project Area. Additionally, the Merged Project Area includes three major private developments
known as the Market Place Development, the Towne Center Project, ,nA tt " Atlantic Times
Square Project.
Mr. Talbot is currently a fifty-percent equity partner in a business named Gateway
Consultants ("Gateway"), which provides development consulting services. Currently, dateway
is providing consulting services to Monterey Park Retail Partners, LLC ("MpRp,,), *-hich is the
developer for the Market Place Development. The Market Place Development consists of a
planned 600,000 square foot retail shopping center on a forty-five acre siie located within
Monterey Park. Additionally, MPRP has an option to purchase the real property upon which the
Market Place Development is proposed to be built. MPRP is seeking a number of intitlements
and agreements with the City and the RDA including, without limitaiion, environmental review
in accordance with the Califomia Environmental Quality Act, processing subdivision maps, a
development agreement with the City, and a disposition and develop-"nt agreement with the
RDA. At this time, none of these entitlements have been approved. The eniitlements are being
processed and will potentially be considered by the City and the RDA within the next 12 months.
The RDA and MPRP have also previously discussed the feasibility of paying for certain
offsite improvements, such as roadway improvements, located adjacent to the Market place
Development. These improvements will provide benefits to the Market Place Development as
well as other adjacent properties. If the RDA does not build the offsite improvements, MpRp
will need to pay for the improvements, which are estimated to cost uppror,i-otely $g,000,000.
While uncertain, it is also possible that decisions related to the Atlaniic Times Square project and
the Towne Center Project may affect the amount of money available for the offsiie
improvements needed for the Market Place Development. To address monetary limitations, the
city is contemplating merging the Atlantic Garvey Project Area with the Merged project Area.
This merger would make more funds available for projects within the newly lormed project area
and provide greater flexibility regarding the use of redevelopment monies.
During the previous 12 months, MPRP has paid Gateway more than $500 for consulting
services. Payments are made on a monthly basis, and you have asked that we presume Mr.
Talbot has received over $500 from Gateway for services provided to MPRp. -you 
,tut" that Mr.
Talbot is not entitled to a completion bonus upon the completion of the Market place
Development. If hired as the city manager, Mr. Talbot has informed the City that he will
immediately resign from his position at Gateway. Upon his resignation, Mr. Talbot will not
receive any further remuneration from the MPRP. In a telephon" "orr"rration on September 21,
2010, you stated that, in resigning from his position with Gateway, Mr. Talbot will divest
himself of any interest he holds in Gateway including his fifty-peicent ownership interest.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her otTicial position to influence a governmental deciiion in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.
Step One: Is the individual a "public official?,,
The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bX1).) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee
or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .." (Section 82048.) If apyointed as the
city manager, Mr. Talbot will be a public official within the meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Is the official making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any collrse of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.\ A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the
official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.3.) If hired as the city manager, Mr. Talbot will be making,
participating in making, or influencing governmental decisions when taking part in cit! decisions
regarding MPRP, private developments within the city, or redevelopment projects.
Step Three: What are the official's economic interests?
Of the economic interests recognized under the Acta, those interests that may be
implicated by your account of the facts are the following:
Business Entity - A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which
he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or in which he or she i.s a
I If a public official's otfice is listed in Section 81200 (*87200 filers" include city managers) and he or she
has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: ( I ) immediately prior to the
discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of
the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation 18702.5(bX I )(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself
or herselt and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. Foi closed sessions,
consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member ofthe public regarding personal interests, special rules tbund
in Regulation 18702.5. subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)
' our analysis is limited to the economic interests you have identit'ied.
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section
87103(a) and (d); Regulation 18703.1(a) and (b).)
Source of Income - A public official has an economic interest in any source of income,
including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within the 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) "Income" is defined to include a pro rata
share of the income of any business entity or trlrst in which the official (or his or her ipouse)
owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest. (Section SZdfOlay.y
Personal Finances - A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is known as the "personal
financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 1g703.5.)
Provided that Mr. Talbot immediately divests himself of any interesr in Gateway if hired
as the city manager, no economic interests are implicated by your accollnt of the facts for
decisions more than 12 months from Mr. Talbot's appointment. Barring any other economic
interests that Mr. Talbot may have, the Act does not prohibit Mr. Talboi from making,
participating in making, or influencing a decision by the City or the RDA affecting either
Gateway or MPRP, after 12 months of leaving Gateway, so long as he has received no income
from either source during the l2-month period.
Notwithstanding decisions made more than 12 months after Mr. Talbot has divested
himself of his interest in Gateway, you have also asked for assistance regarding Mr. Talbot,s
participation in decisions upon his appointment that may affect Gateway or nAFnp by affecting
the amount of public funds available for the Market Place Developmeri. Th.r" decisions
potentially include decisions regarding the Atlantic Times Square Project, the Towne Center
Project, or the merging of the Atlantic-Garvey Project Area with the Merged project Area. We
continue our analysis in regards to Mr. Talbot's participation in decisio6 upo., his appointment
but within 12 months of divesting himself of his interest in Gateway
Gateway and MPRP:
Provided that Mr. Talbot divests himself of any interest in Gateway immediately upon his
appointment, he will not have an investment interest in Gateway. Howevir, from the facts
provided, Mr. Talbot has received income of $500 dollars or more from Gateway for services
provided to MPRP. Thus, Mr. Talbot has an economic interest in Gateway as a source of
income. Additionally, because Mr. Talbot held a fifty-percent ownership interest in Gateway
prior to divesting his interest, any source of income to Gateway is also asource of income to Mr.
Talbot if his pro rata share of the income derived by Gateway from the source aggregates to $500
or more in the l2 months prior to the govemmental decision. (Sections 82030(a) -a gZtOl("1.)
Based Llpon the facts provided, Mr. Talbot also has an economic interest in MpRp as a source of
income.
Personal Finances:
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances. A
governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will result in
the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regularion 18703.5.)
Step Four: Are the official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
Gateway and MPRP:
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when that business entity or source oi in"o*e,
either directly or by an agent:
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject oia
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."
Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in governmental
decisions are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 18704.1(b), 18705.1(a)(2), and
Regulation 18705.3(b).) Under the facts you have presented, there is no indication that either
Gateway or MPRP are directly involved in decisions other than decisions related to the Market
Place Development.
Personal Finances
As for an official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, an official's
economic interest in his or her personal finances is deemed to be directly involved in the
governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect on the economic interest. (Regulation
18704.5.)
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
official's economic interests?
Materiality
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to each economic interest.
Gateway and MpRp:
For economic interests in business entities directly involved in a decision, including
business entities that are a source of income to an officiai, the materiality standard is given at
Regulation 18705.1(b), which states, "the financial effects of a govemmlnt decision on a
business entity which is directly involved in the govemment deJision is presumed to be
material." This presumption of materiality *ay be rebutted only by p.oof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have aiy-financial effect on the
business entity.
For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision, including
business entities that are a source of income to an official, ihe materiality standard is given at
Regulation 18705.1(c). The thresholds for materiality under this regulaiion vary with the size of
the business. While you have not provided 
-facts relaiing to the size of either Gateway or MpRp,
Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) provides that the financial .fflt of a governmental decision on a
business, not publicly traded and relatively modest in economic size, is material if it is
reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing.*f"nr". fo. o
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 o, *or"; or,
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,
Mr' Talbot should consult Regulation 18705.1(c) to ensure that he identifies the
materiality threshold actually appropriate to both cateway and MpRp.
In addition to the materiality standards for sources of income described above, there is a
separate materiality standard that applies in cases where there is a ..nexus,, between duties owed
to a source of income and to the official's public agency. when a public official is paid by
another entity to accomplish some action that is within the official's public decisionmaking
authority, any financial effect on the solrrce of income is presumed material. The .,nexlls test,, is
set out at Regulation 18705.3(c), which provides the following:
"(c) Nexus. Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who
is a source of income to a public official is deemed material if the pubtic official
receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose *t i"t would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."
Personal Finances:
Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in Regulation 1g705.5(a),
which provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal
finances is materiar if it is at least $250 in any l2-montL period. when
determining whether a governmental decisio, hor o material financial effect on a
public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a
financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the
official, nor a financial effect on the g.o.r i.re.rues, expenses, or value of assets
and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered."
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each of his or
her economic interests, the next step is determining whethei it is "reason'ably foreseeable,, that
the standard will be met. A material financial erre"t on an economic interesi is ..reasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met
as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) However, an effect need not be
certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility.
(ln re Tlnrner (1975) 1 FppC Ops. l9g.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Tltorner, sypra,at l9g.)
Because the Commission does not act as. a finder of fact in providing advice (ln re oglesby
(r975) I FPPC ops. 7l), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determinarion that
must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.
Nonetheless' you have provided facts indicating that decisions regarding the Market
Place Development will have a reasonably foreseeable-material financial effect on Mr. Talbot,s
economic interests in both Gateway and MPRP as sources of income. First, both Gateway and
MPRP appear to be directly involved in decisions regarding the Market place Development.
Second, there also appears to be a nexlrs between ur. rauit's duties as a consultant and duties
should he be hired as the city manager. Thus, the financial effect on Mr. Talbot,s economic
interests in Gateway and MPRP is presumed material under both Regulations 1g705.1(b) and
18705'3(c). Even baring any other economic interests, Mr. Talbot may not make, participate in
making, or influen^ce the decisions regarding the Market Place Develoirnent unless he can rebut
the presumption of materiality by demonstrating that there will be absolutely zo financial effect,
not even a single-penny's effect, on his economic interests in either Gateway or MpRp.
Additionally, you have stated that decisions related to the Atlantic Times square project
and the Towne Center Project, as well as the merging of the Atlantic Garvey project Area with
the Merged Project Area, may affect the amount of puUf i" funds available for the Market place
Development' Based upon these facts, it also appears that decisions related to these two projects,
and the merging of the redevelopment project oreur, will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Mr. Talbot's economic-interests in both Gateway ana vtpnp as sources of
income' As a previously paid consultant for the Market Place Development, any financial effect
on Mr' Talbot's economic interests in Gateway or MPRP as sources of income is deemed
material under the nexus test because he has received income in the previous 12 months to
achieve a goal or purpose that would be achieved, defeated, aided, oi hindered by decisions
related to these two projects or the merging of the project areas. Even barring any other
economic interests, Mr. Talbot may not make, particiiate in making, or influJnce the decisions
related to the Atlantic Times Square Project and the To*n" Center-iro.lect, as well as the
merging of the Atlantic Garvey Project Area with the Merged project Area, unless (l) he can
rebut the presumption of materiality by demonstrating that'there will be absolutely no financial
effect, not even a single-penny's effect, on his ,"onoiri" interests in either Gateway or MpRp or
!?]^.1":*.1:l:iti* segmented from decisions regarding the Marker place Devetopment as
orscusseo Delow."
Steps Seven and Eight: Does the governmental decision come within any exception to the
confl ict-of-interest rules?
Segmentation
The commission has advised that "[u]nder certain circumstances, a public official
disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions provided that the
official's participation does not affect the decision in which he or she has a conflict of interest.,,
(In re owen (1976) 2 FPPC ops. 77.) [n these cases, related decisions -uy U" r"gmented from
the decision in which the official has a conflict of interest so long as the o.iion taken in the
5 unless segmented.from.decisions regarding the Market Place Development, Mr. Talbot is also likely
prohibited from making, participating in making, or irifluencing decisions related ro rhe Arlantic Times square
Project and the Towne Center Project, as well ai the merging o'f the Atiantic Garvey project Area with the Merged
Project Area, solely becauseof his likely disqualification t.J* decisions regardingihe Market place Developmenr.
In the past, the Commission has advised that i'inextricably intenelated" decisions Lay not be considered separately
unless the decisions can be segmented, as discussed below. tsee generail y yangAdvice Letter, No. I-06- l9g ; stone
Advice Lerter, No. A.-06-007; Barker Advice Letter, A_05; and HullAdvice Letrer, No. 4_04_052.)
related decision will not effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the decision
in which the official has a conflict of interest.
Regulation 18709 provides the rules for the "segmentation" of a governmental decision:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, prorided all of the
following conditions apply:
"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented
from the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's participation in any way; and
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has
been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a
reopening of or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official
was disqualified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably
interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively determinl, affirm,
nullify, or alter the result of another decision.',
Based upon the facts you have provided, it may be possible to segment decisions related
to the Atlantic Times Square Project and the Towne Centei Project, o. *.ll as the merging of the
Atlantic Garvey Project Area with the Merged Project Area, from decisions regarding ihe-trrtarket
Place Development. However, this is a viable option only to the extent that de-cisionJ regarding
the Market Place Development are considered first, without Mr. Talbot's participation, ild
ftiture decisions regarding the other projects, and the merging of the redevelopment project areas,
will not act to determine, affirm, nullify, or alter a previously made decision ielated to the
Market Place Development. If you need additional assistance regarding the segmentation of a
specific governmental decision, it is advisable that you seek further advice.
Public Generally & Legally Required participation
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally. (Section
87103; Regulation I 8707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take part
in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the
"legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very specific
circumstances where the govemment agency would bi paratyzed from acting. (section g7101;
Regulation 18708.)
However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally,, or the
"le.gally required participation" exceptions are applicable to Mr. Talbot-,s circu-mstances, so we
will not address them further.

2. Upon his appointment, may Mr. Talbot take part in decisions related to other private

3. If the City of Monterey Park (the "City") determines before Mr. Talbot is appointed
city manager that the City will not spend funds on the Market Place Development for ii least 12
months after Mr. Talbot's appointment, may Mr. Talbot take part in decisions upon his

CONCLUSION: 1. Provided there are no reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on any other
economic interests Mr. Talbot may have, he may take part in decisions regarding the Market
Place Development after the elapse of 12 months from his appointrnent ro long as he divests
himself of any interest in his consulting business immediately upon his appointment and receives
no remuneration of any kind from either the business or the developer of the project after his
appointment.
2. Upon his appointment, Mr. Talbot may not take part in decisions related to other
private projects or the merging of two redevelopment project areas that may affect the amount of
public funds available for the Market Place Development, within l2 monthi of receiving income
from either his former business or the developer of the Market Place Development, unless the
decisions can be segmented as discussed below.
3. Even if the City determines before Mr. Talbot is appointed city manager that it will
not spend funds on the Market Place Development for at least 12 months after Mr. Talbot,s
appointment, Mr. Talbot may not take part in decisions related to other private projects or the
merging of two redevelopment project areas that may affect the amouniof pubfic funds available
for the Market Place Development, within 12 months of receiving income fiom either his former
business or the developer of the Market Place Development, unless the decisions can be
segmented as discussed below.

Nothing in this letter should be constmed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature and you have not limited your question to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your reqllest as one for informal assistance.2 Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act.
